About This Dashboard

This dashboard explores Magic: The Gathering card data sourced from the Scryfall bulk data API — a comprehensive, regularly updated database of every MTG card ever printed.

What is in the dataset?

Each row represents a unique card (identified by its oracle_id), keeping only the cheapest available printing when multiple exist. The key variables we analyze are:

Variable Description
name Card name
cmc Converted Mana Cost — total mana to cast
colors Color identity (W, U, B, R, G)
type_line Card type (Creature, Instant, etc.)
rarity Common, Uncommon, Rare, Mythic, Special
price Lowest USD market price across all printings
legalities Whether the card is legal in Standard, Modern, or Commander
What does this dashboard explore?
  • Price Analysis — How are card prices distributed? What drives value?
  • Mana & Color — How complex are cards by mana cost and color?
  • Format Legality — What share of cards are legal in each format?
  • Card Browser — Search and filter the full card database yourself.

Price Distribution (USD) — log scale shows the full spread
Key Price Findings

The chart uses a log scale on the x-axis — each step to the right is 10× more expensive. This reveals the true shape of the distribution, which is impossible to see on a linear scale because so many cards cost under $1.

The bell-shaped curve on a log scale tells us that card prices follow a log-normal distribution — most cards cluster around a central value, with tails of very cheap and very expensive cards on either side.

Summary statistics:
  • Mean price: $2.85 — pulled up by expensive outliers
  • Median price: $0.21 — a better measure of the “typical” card
  • 82% of cards cost under $1
  • Only 1% cost $50 or more

The large gap between mean and median confirms that a small number of extremely valuable cards skew the average significantly upward.

Average Price by Rarity
Average Price by Card Type
What is Converted Mana Cost (CMC)?

Every MTG card requires mana to cast — the game’s primary resource. Converted Mana Cost (CMC) is the total amount of mana required, regardless of color. A CMC of 1 is a cheap, fast card; a CMC of 7+ is a powerful late-game spell. Understanding CMC helps reveal the design philosophy behind the game: aggressive strategies rely on low-CMC cards, while control decks can afford higher costs. The scatter plot below lets you explore whether higher mana cost actually translates to higher market value.

CMC Distribution — How Complex Are Cards?
Average CMC by Card Type
Price vs. CMC — Hover over points for card details (sample of 5,000 cards, prices ≤ $100)
MTG’s Major Competitive Formats

MTG has several formats that determine which cards are legal to play competitively. Standard rotates yearly, only allowing cards from recent sets — keeping the meta fresh but making cards obsolete. Modern allows cards from 2003 onward, creating a much larger card pool. Commander is the most popular casual format, where nearly every non-banned card is legal and players build 100-card decks around a legendary creature. Because Standard bans more cards, legal cards there tend to be more recently printed and often more expensive due to active demand.

Standard Legality
Modern Legality
Commander Legality
Average Price of Legal Cards by Format
What the Legality Data Shows
  • Only 13% of all cards are Standard-legal, reflecting how aggressively that format rotates old sets out.
  • 67% are Modern-legal — a much larger pool drawing from over 20 years of card printings.
  • 94% are Commander-legal, making it the most inclusive format by far.

Standard-legal cards command the highest average prices because they are in active competitive demand. Once a card rotates out of Standard, its price typically drops unless it sees play in Modern or Commander.

This shows that format legality is one of the strongest drivers of card value — not just rarity alone.

Key Findings

This dashboard analyzed 32,803 unique Magic: The Gathering cards sourced from the Scryfall database. Here is a summary of what we found:

Price
  • Card prices are extremely right-skewed. The median price is only $0.21, but the mean is $2.85 due to a small number of very expensive cards pulling the average up.
  • 82% of all cards cost under $1, while just 1% cost $50 or more.
  • Mythic rarity cards have the highest average price, followed by Rare. Common cards are nearly worthless on the secondary market.
  • Planeswalkers have the highest average price by card type, likely because they are almost always printed at Mythic rarity.
Mana Cost & Complexity
  • Most cards have a CMC between 1 and 5, with CMC 2–3 being the most common range. This reflects the game’s design principle of rewarding efficient, lower-cost cards.
  • Lands have a CMC of 0 (they do not require mana to play), while Sorceries and Creatures tend to have higher average CMC than Instants.
  • There is no strong linear relationship between CMC and price — a high-mana-cost card is not necessarily more valuable.
Format Legality
  • Standard is the most restrictive format, with only 13% of cards legal.
  • Standard-legal cards carry the highest average price, confirming that competitive demand drives value more than rarity alone.
  • Commander is the most inclusive format and the most popular casual format, accepting 94% of all cards.
